note: 在曼达洛人的第 2 季结局中，这位名义上的孤独赏金猎人完成了他的任务，将格罗古（以前称为尤达宝宝）交付给绝地张开的双臂。但不仅仅是任何绝地武士。一位年轻的卢克·天行者出现在兴奋中，然后是粉丝们的失望，他们对他的 VFX 沉重的表情感到惊讶。进入名为 Shamook 的 YouTube 用户，他的 The Mandalorian deepfake 于 12 月出版，已获得近 200 万次观看，以改善用于使 Mark Hamill 衰老的视觉效果。太棒了，Shamook 然后与 Lucasfilm 及其视觉效果部门 Industrial Light and Magic 获得了新的演出机会。 “你们中的一些人可能已经知道，我几个月前加入了 ILM/Lucasfilms，但没有时间制作任何新的 YouTube 内容，”Shamook 在最近一段视频的评论部分写道。 “现在我已经适应了我的工作，上传应该会再次开始增加。它们仍然会很慢，但希望不会相隔几个月。” “[工业光与魔法]一直在寻找才华横溢的艺术家，实际上已经聘请了在线角色'Shamook'的艺术家，”卢卡斯影业的一位代表在一份声明中说。 “在过去的几年里，ILM 一直在投资机器学习和人工智能，以此作为制作引人注目的视觉效果作品的一种手段，随着技术的进步，看到这个领域的势头正在增强，这真是太棒了。” Deepfake 视频使用人工智能使人看起来正在做或说一些他们从未做过的事情，就像这些 Tom Cruise 的 deepfakes 一样。在不那么令人不安的领域，改善电影视觉效果的深度伪造似乎是你电影制作简历的一个很好的补充。
title: 卢卡斯影业聘请 Deepfake YouTuber 修复曼达洛人
title_en: Lucasfilm Hires Deepfake YouTuber Who Fixed The Mandalorian

note: 美国已将吴唐氏专辑《少林黄飞鸿》的唯一副本出售给一位匿名买家。出售的价格也被保密。这张专辑之前由臭名昭著的“Pharma Bro”马丁·什克雷利（Martin Shkreli）拥有，他将一种救命药物的价格提高了 5,000%，后来被判犯有证券欺诈罪并被判处七年徒刑。作为 740 万美元没收判决的一部分，他于 2018 年被迫将其交给美国。纽约东区代理美国检察官 Jacquelyn M. Kasulis 今天宣布了这一消息。 “Shkreli 已被追究责任，并为撒谎和窃取投资者以致富而付出了代价，”Kasulis 说。 “随着今天这张独一无二的专辑的销售，他的没收款项现已完成。” Shkreli 于 2015 年在拍卖会上以 200 万美元的价格购买了这张 31 首曲目的专辑。它被宣传为“独特的艺术作品”，没有实体或数字复制品，包括从雷德曼到基拉米的更广泛的吴唐家族的出现。在得知是 Shkreli 购买了这张专辑后，Wu-Tang 成员 RZA 告诉彭博社，该组织决定将“很大一部分收益捐给慈善机构”。
title: 美国政府出售“Pharma Bro” Martin Shkreli 独一无二的武当家族专辑
title_en: 'Pharma Bro' Martin Shkreli's One-of-a-Kind Wu-Tang Clan Album Sold By US Government

默认情况下，Instagram 已将 16 岁以下的新帐户设为私有，因此只有获得批准的关注者才能看到帖子和“喜欢”或评论。它说，测试表明，当私人设置是默认设置时，只有五分之一的人选择了公共帐户。现有账户持有人将收到通知，“强调转为私人的好处”。但 Instagram 也表示，它正在推动面向 13 岁以下青少年的新应用程序，尽管遭到了一些团体的强烈反对。母公司 Facebook 表示：“现实情况是，他们已经上线，并且没有万无一失的方法来阻止人们歪曲他们的年龄，我们希望打造专门为他们设计、由父母和监护人管理的体验。”即将出台的在线安全法案让科技巨头有责任确保采取足够的保护措施，以防止儿童访问可能有害的内容。但该系统依赖于帐户中列出的年龄——年轻用户可能会为了避免此类限制而撒谎。
该公司表示：“从历史上看，我们要求年轻人在注册 Instagram 时在公共帐户或私人帐户之间进行选择，但我们最近的研究表明，他们更喜欢更私密的体验。” Instagram 表示，它的另一项变化 - 防止帐户显示“潜在的可疑行为”，例如最近被阻止发送消息或关注儿童的帐户 - 将使他们“难以为某些成年人找到”。同时，“更谨慎的方法”将使广告商能够仅根据年龄、性别和位置而不是兴趣和网络浏览习惯来定位儿童。在为一般的有针对性的广告及其对某些类型的选择退出功能进行辩护时，Instagram 表示：“我们从青年倡导者那里听说，年轻人可能没有能力做出这些决定。”
note_en:
Instagram has made new under-16s' accounts private by default so only approved followers can see posts and "like" or comment.
 Tests showed only one in five opted for a public account when the private setting was the default, it said.
 And existing account holders would be sent a notification "highlighting the benefits" of switching to private.
 But Instagram also said it was pushing ahead with new apps for under-13s, despite  a backlash from some groups.
 "The reality is that they are already online and, with no foolproof way to stop people from misrepresenting their age, we want to build experiences designed specifically for them, managed by parents and guardians," parent company Facebook said.
    The upcoming Online Safety Bill puts the onus on technology giants to ensure sufficient safeguards to prevent children accessing potentially harmful content.
     But the system relies on the age listed in the account - something younger users may lie about to avoid such restrictions.
   "Historically, we asked young people to choose between a public account or a private account when they signed up for Instagram but our recent research showed that they appreciate a more private experience," the company said.
  Another of its changes - preventing accounts showing "potentially suspicious behaviour" such as recently having been blocked from messaging or following children's accounts - would make them "difficult to find for certain adults", Instagram said.
 Meanwhile, "a more precautionary approach" will see advertisers able to target children based on only age, gender, and location, rather than interests and web-browsing habits.
 And while defending targeted ads in general and the optout features it has for some types, Instagram said: "We have heard from youth advocates that young people may not be well equipped to make these decisions."
title: Instagram 默认将 16 岁以下的帐户设为私有
title_en: Instagram Makes Under-16s' Accounts Private By Default

note: 自从在 I/O 2019 期间推出以来，Jetpack Compose 显然注定要成为 Android UI 开发的规定方法。经过两年多的公开开发，它达到了许多开发人员一直在等待的里程碑：正式 1.0 版本。除了 Android Studio Arctic Fox 的稳定版本外，Jetpack Compose 还可以用于生产代码。谷歌在 3 月份发布了 1.0 的测试版，给了它几个月的时间来识别和修复最终的错误。这包括一些最近添加的功能，例如 Compose Preview 和 Deploy Preview。前者允许您在许多不同的状态下查看 Compose UI 的实时渲染，例如明暗模式或不同的字体缩放。 Deploy Preview 更面向功能，允许您在不完全重新启动应用程序的情况下推送更新的代码。这两者都需要 Android Studio Arctic Fox，它今天也升级为稳定版。在将 Jetpack Compose 集成到他们的应用程序之前，许多开发人员一直在等待稳定的 1.0 版本。由于该框架允许在现有视图中使用 Compose，或将现有视图包含在 Composable 中，因此它使开发人员能够以任何速度将 Jetpack Compose 集成到他们的项目中——无需进行大的转换，甚至无需重写任何代码。 Compose 还可以与其他 Jetpack 库顺利集成，以实现 LiveData 和 Navigation 等功能。所有 UI 元素均采用 Material Design 设计并支持主题化，因此您可以自定义应用程序的外观，丰富的动画 API 可以轻松传达活动。虽然最终的 1.0 版本是一个重要的里程碑，但它显然并不标志着 Jetpack Compose 开发的结束。谷歌正在发布将添加到框架中的即将到来的特性和功能的公共路线图。密切关注它，看看接下来会发生什么。今天还推出了稳定版 Android Studio Arctic Fox。正如您所料，今天的更新具有常规的错误修复功能。但是，谷歌强调了最近的新增功能，例如新的后台任务管理器，可用于分析应用程序中的后台工作人员，以便您更好地了解性能问题。它加入了一系列其他最近添加的功能，例如简化的 Wear OS 配对和模拟器支持、文字的实时编辑、Compose 的布局检查器等。可以在 Android 开发人员站点上找到稳定版本，其中包含 Jetpack Compose 的最终版本。 （注意：更新链接可能需要几分钟时间。）
note_en:
Since its  introduction during I/O 2019, Jetpack Compose was obviously destined to become the prescribed method of Android UI development. After more than two years in public development, it has hit the milestone many developers have been waiting for: an official 1.0 release. Alongside a stable release of Android Studio Arctic Fox,  Jetpack Compose is ready for use in production code.
  Google released a beta of 1.0  back in March, giving it a few months for final bugs to be identified and fixed. This includes some of the recently added features, like Compose Preview and Deploy Preview. The former allows you to see a live rendering of your Compose UIs in a number of different states, like light and dark modes, or with different font scaling. Deploy Preview is more functionally oriented and allows you to push updated code without completely restarting your app. Both of these require Android Studio Arctic Fox, which also saw a promotion to stable today.
  Many developers have been waiting for a stable 1.0 release before integrating Jetpack Compose into their apps. Since the framework allows Compose to be used within existing views, or for existing views to be contained inside of a Composable, it enables developers to integrate Jetpack Compose into their projects at any pace — there's no need to commit to big transitions or even rewriting any code. Compose also integrates smoothly with other Jetpack libraries for things like LiveData and Navigation. All of the UI elements are styled with Material Design and support theming so you can customize the look of your app, and a rich animation API makes it easy to convey activity.
 While a final 1.0 release is a major milestone, it obviously doesn't mark the end of development on Jetpack Compose. Google is publishing a  public roadmap to upcoming features and capabilities that will be added to the framework. Keep an eye on it to see what's coming next.
   Also rolling out today is the stable release of Android Studio Arctic Fox. As you might expect, today's update features the usual run of bug fixes. However, Google is highlighting recent additions like the new Background Task Manager, which can be used to analyze background workers in your app so you can get a better view of performance issues. It joins a slew of other recent additions like simplified Wear OS pairing and emulator support, live editing of literals, a layout inspector for Compose, and more.
 The stable releases can be found on the  Android developer site and it contains the final release of Jetpack Compose. (Note: It may take a few minutes for the links to be updated.)
title: 经过两年的公开开发，谷歌发布了第一个稳定版本的 Jetpack Compose，一个 Android 的 UI 框架
title_en: Google releases the first stable build of Jetpack Compose, a UI framework for Android, after two years in public development

语言学习公司 Duolingo 周三在纳斯达克上市首日收盘上涨 36%，股票代码为“DUOL”。股价收于 139.01 美元，使公司市值接近 50 亿美元。该公司周二以每股 102 美元的价格定价 370 万股，高于其最初的 85 美元至 95 美元的目标区间。根据 PitchBook 的数据，它以 37 亿美元的隐含估值筹集了 5.21 亿美元，高于去年约 24 亿美元的私人市场估值。这家总部位于匹兹堡的公司在其 IPO 招股说明书中披露，去年的年化收入增长了 129%，达到 1.617 亿美元。文件显示，该公司截至 3 月 31 日的季度收入为 5540 万美元，比去年增长 97%，而净亏损扩大了六倍多，达到 1350 万美元。 Duolingo 提供 95 门课程，涵盖 40 种不同的语言——从世界上最常用的语言（如西班牙语、法语和意大利语）到濒危语言（如夏威夷语、纳瓦霍语和苏格兰盖尔语）。许多人不知道的是，世界上最受欢迎的教育应用程序最初是联合创始人 Severin Hacker 和 Luis von Ahn 的一个计算机科学项目，在尝试同时翻译整个互联网的同时教人们外语。 Von Ahn 是 CAPTCHA 和 ReCAPTCHA 发明背后的开发者之一，它们用于区分人类和机器。 “我们是世界上最流行的学习语言的方式，”联合创始人兼首席执行官 Luis von Ahn 周三早上在股票开始交易前在 CNBC 的“TechCheck”上说。 “我们的大部分收入来自订阅。”该公司声称每月有 4000 万活跃用户和超过 5 亿次下载。它高度依赖于通过移动应用商店的分发。该公司表示，2020 年，其 51% 的收入来自 Apple 的 App Store，19% 的收入来自 Google Play 商店，并且是每家商店教育类别中收入最高的应用程序。 Duolingo 还声称在谷歌搜索中很受欢迎，其名称的搜索量是“学习西班牙语”一词的 9 倍多。
除了其核心平台，该公司还创建了 Duolingo English Test，这是一种被全球 2,000 多所大学和机构接受的语言认证选项。冯·安说：“如果你住在美国，你可能不知道这一点，但全世界的人们不得不参加测试以证明他们懂英语，这花费了数十亿美元。” “在大流行期间，我们得到了很多机构接受我们的测试，”他补充说，并声称包括斯坦福大学在内的前 20 所大学中有 17 所接受 Duolingo 的在线测试作为国际学生英语水平的证明。 Von Ahn 还表示，这部分业务目前占收入的 9%，他预计这一数字将继续增长。去年，该公司推出了 Duolingo ABC，这是一款面向 3 至 6 岁儿童的免费英语识字应用程序。该公司原计划在今年晚些时候推出该应用程序，但由于冠状病毒大流行，决定提前发布，以帮助父母远程工作和在家上学他们的孩子。该应用程序没有广告，并提供 300 多个教授基本阅读和写作技能的简短课程。 Kleiner Perkins、Union Square Ventures 和 Alphabet 的独立增长基金 CapitalG 是 Duolingo 最大的风险投资投资者，准备从此次发行中获得可观的收益。高盛、艾伦公司、Evercore ISI 和巴克莱是主要承销商。 Duolingo 是三度入选 CNBC Disruptor 50 的公司，最近在 2020 年榜单上排名第 42 位。根据复兴资本的数据，2021 年美国 IPO 的收益已达到 890 亿美元，比去年同期增长 232%。从年初至今，市场筹集的资金已经处于创纪录的水平，预计将超过 2000 年在互联网繁荣时期筹集的 970 亿美元的全年历史新高，根据文艺复兴。从居家科技到医疗保健创新者再到电子商务公司，公司都在利用蓬勃发展的股市，在对经济重新开放的乐观情绪的支持下不断刷新其记录。 IPO 热潮也恰逢散户投资者的崛起，他们渴望拥有自己最喜欢的公司的一部分。
根据复兴资本的数据，2021 年共有 250 宗 IPO 定价，比去年同期增长 191%，已经超过 2020 年的 218 宗 IPO 总数。自两个月前公布 2021 年榜单以来，已有 9 家 CNBC Disruptor 50 公司上市，其中包括 SoFi、23andMe 和 SentinelOne。 Robinhood 是一家五次登上 2021 年榜单的 Disruptor 50 公司，定于周四在纳斯达克开始交易。注册我们每周一次的原创时事通讯，它超出了列表范围，让您更深入地了解 Disruptor 50 公司，以及在经济的各个领域继续创新的创始人。
note_en:
Shares of language-learning company  Duolingo closed up 36% in its market debut Wednesday on the Nasdaq, going public under the ticket symbol "DUOL." Shares closed at $139.01, giving the company a market capitalization of nearly $5 billion.
 The company priced 3.7 million shares at $102 apiece on Tuesday, above its initial $85 to $95 target range. It raised $521 million at an implied $3.7 billion valuation, up from last year's private market valuation of about $2.4 billion, according to PitchBook data.
  In its IPO prospectus, the Pittsburgh-based company disclosed annualized revenue growth of 129% last year to $161.7 million. The company brought in $55.4 million in revenue for the quarter ended March 31 — a 97% jump from last year — while net losses widened more than six-fold to $13.5 million, according to the filing.
 Duolingo offers 95 courses across 40 distinct languages — from the world's most spoken, such as Spanish, French and Italian, to endangered languages like Hawaiian, Navajo and Scottish Gaelic.
 What many don't know is that the world's most popular education app began as a computer science project for co-founders Severin Hacker and Luis von Ahn, teaching people foreign languages while trying to simultaneously translate the entire internet. Von Ahn was one of the developers behind the invention of CAPTCHA and ReCAPTCHA, which are used to distinguish humans from machines.
 "We're the most popular way to learn languages in the world," co-founder and CEO Luis von Ahn said on CNBC's " TechCheck" Wednesday morning before shares started trading. "We make the majority of our revenue through subscriptions."
  The company claims 40 million monthly active users and more than 500 million downloads. It's highly dependent on distribution through mobile app stores. In 2020, the company says it derived 51% of revenue from  Apple's App Store and 19% from the  Google Play Store, and was the top-grossing app in the education category for each store. Duolingo also claims popularity in Google searches, with more than nine times as many searches for its name as for the phrase "learn Spanish."
  In addition to its core platform, the company created the Duolingo English Test, a language certification option accepted by more than 2,000 universities and institutions worldwide.
 "If you live in the United States you may not know about this, but worldwide billions of dollars are spent by people having to take tests to prove that they know English," von Ahn said. "During the pandemic we got a lot of institutions accepting our tests," he added, claiming that 17 of the top 20 universities, including Stanford, accept Duolingo's online test as proof of English proficiency for international students. Von Ahn also said that this part of the business currently accounts for 9% of revenue and he expects that figure to continue growing.
 Last year the company launched Duolingo ABC, a free English literacy app for children ages 3 to 6. The company originally planned to unveil the app later this year, but due to the coronavirus pandemic decided to release it early to help parents working remotely and homeschooling their children. The app is free from ads and features more than 300 short lessons that teach basic reading and writing skills.
  Kleiner Perkins, Union Square Ventures and  Alphabet's independent growth fund CapitalG are among Duolingo's biggest venture capital investors, poised to amass significant gains from the offering.  Goldman Sachs, Allen & Company,  Evercore ISI and Barclays were the lead underwriters.
 Duolingo is a three-time  CNBC Disruptor 50 company that most recently  ranked No. 42 on the 2020 list.
  Proceeds from U.S. IPOs have reached $89 billion in 2021, a 232% jump from the same period last year, according to data from Renaissance Capital. For the year-to-date period, the market is already at a record level in terms of funds raised, and it is expected to surpass the full-year all-time high of $97 billion raised in 2000 amid the dot-com boom, according to Renaissance.
 Companies from stay-at-home tech to health-care innovators to e-commerce players are taking advantage of a booming stock market that keeps  refreshing its record on the back of optimism toward the economic reopening. The IPO boom also coincides with the rising force of retail investors who are eager to own a piece of their favorite companies.
 A total of 250 IPOs have priced in 2021, up 191% from the same period last year and already beating 2020′s total number of IPOs at 218, according to Renaissance Capital.
 Nine CNBC Disruptor 50 companies have become publicly listed since the 2021 list was revealed just two months ago, including  SoFi,  23andMe and  SentinelOne. Robinhood, a five-time Disruptor 50 company  that topped the 2021 list, is slated to begin trading Thursday on the Nasdaq.

note_en:
Market research has been driving strategic decisions for companies for decades. However, the tools and models that have been predominantly used have not adjusted to reflect today’s digital age and today’s consumer environment.   Suzy is a modern, digitally-native enterprise market research platform built for the sophisticated needs of today’s brands.  Offering on-demand insights, the company has shifted the pricing paradigm away from cost per response to a SaaS offering with licenses to customers, providing more flexibility and predictability. By integrating technology onto the platform, Suzy is able to deliver actionable insights much faster in an easy-to-use end-to-end cost-effective solution.
 AlleyWatch caught up with CEO, serial entrepreneur, and Founder  Matt Britton to learn more about the genesis of Suzy from his agency days, the company’s strategic plans, latest round of funding, which brings the total funding raised to $104.1M, and much, much more.
  This was a Series D round. The total raised was $50M.  Lead Investor is  H.I.G. Capital. Existing investors that participated included:  Foundry Group, Rho Ventures, BDMI, and  Triangle Peak Partners.
  Suzy is an enterprise software platform that enables its customers to conduct on-demand “direct to consumer” market research, both quantitative and qualitative.
  Suzy was a pivot from the original business called Crowdtap (which itself was incubated within my former ad agency MRY, acquired by Publicis Group). Crowdtap had built a national network of users who were earning incentives to create and share content on behalf of brands, as it turns out. However, the earliest Crowdtap customers were hacking the tool for what they believed was its most potent user case, real-time consumer insights. After I rejoined Crowdtap as CEO in 2017 it became clear to me that there was a massive opportunity to focus on the insights use case, which was the launch of Suzy in 2018.
  Suzy is the only truly vertically integrated market research tool in the market combining advanced research tools with a proprietary consumer panel and support with specialized research services to meet the growing needs of our client base.
  Suzy is focused on servicing large enterprises with complex research needs and a focus on the evolving consumer. The enterprise U.S. research market is estimated to be over $25B annually.
  Suzy sells annual licenses to its customers, which allows them to unlock its research tools and proprietary audience. License sizes range from $50K/year to upwards of $2M/year.
  The pandemic quickly unraveled nearly every closely held belief about the consumer, attitudes, and habits. The seismic shift we saw in 2020 shined a spotlight on the importance of extracting consumer feedback early and often at every stage of the product market lifecycle, from innovation to foundational learning, to user feedback and experience management. Overall, the pandemic was an accelerator to trends we always thought would unfold in the market research space.
  As this Series D financing was a later stage funding round in a red-hot market, it wasn’t too difficult to get in front of a large group of diversified investors. Moreover, pitching over Zoom made us far more efficient at casting a wide net as we attempted to uncover the right fit for Suzy at this stage. My biggest takeaway is that the VC world still embraces a herd mentality, and few forward thinkers truly understood our vision and potential in the market.
  We encountered the biggest roadblocks because Suzy was very much a “tweener” between a high growth/high burn company and a low growth/lower burn company. In addition, our history of pivoting from Crowdtap made the story a bit messier than some investors wanted to entertain. I also found that since our management team isn’t full of former Facebook/Google execs, we aren’t seen as a risk-free option for many investors. Lastly, many investors just don’t believe in the consumer insights/market research space, which was surprising to me given its growing importance.

In addition, our history of pivoting from Crowdtap made the story a bit messier than some investors wanted to entertain. I also found that since our management team isn’t full of former Facebook/Google execs, we aren’t seen as a risk-free option for many investors. Lastly, many investors just don’t believe in the consumer insights/market research space, which was surprising to me given its growing importance.
  Suzy has a killer product, a world-class customer roster, a proven and & experienced team, and top-tier SaaS unit economics. All of these elements proved vital in securing our funding.
  The next six months are really about scaling our organization to meet our 2022 goals. Ultimately this comes down to acquiring top-tier talent in all areas of our business, including engineer, sales, marketing, customer success, and operations. As a CEO, my core mandate has just shifted from fundraising to hiring.
 What advice can you offer companies in New York that do not have a fresh injection of capital in the bank?
 Focus on your unit economics. VCs almost always will give your metrics to an analyst plugging them into a template to see how they benchmark across competitors. Post Series A, if your unit economics aren’t at least in the top quartile, it will be challenging for you to raise financing even if you have a great product in a hot space.

Post Series A, if your unit economics aren’t at least in the top quartile, it will be challenging for you to raise financing even if you have a great product in a hot space.
  My personal goal is now the same as when we launched for Suzy to complete an Initial Public Offering on the Nasdaq. My dream since graduating college was to ring the bell for a startup that I was leading, and this is my chance to achieve it.
 In order for me to achieve this goal, Suzy has to continue to scale and maintain its growth rates while doing so. This will require continued product improvements and diving deeper into machine learning and data visualization to continue offering our valued customers the best market research software in the marketplace.
 Ultimately, our business model is straightforward; it’s to connect the “askers” (Customers) to the “tellers” so that we can deliver consumer feedback in aggregate in formats that help drive data-driven decisions. At this point, we know there is tremendous demand for our offering and that we can acquire and grow the best customers in the world. Still, we are in a hyper-competitive environment that keeps us on our toes and forces us to continue to innovate every day.

note_en:
Other investors include South Korean sneaker reselling platform KREAM (another SoftBank Ventures Asia portfolio company), Altos Ventures and JAFCO.
 Launched in 2018, SODA runs  SNKRDUNK, one of Japan’s largest sneaker reselling platforms with about 2.5 million monthly users. Along with its new funding, SODA announced it has acquired rival  Monokabu. SODA said that the deal means its share of Japan’s sneaker resale industry is now 80%, making it the market leader by far.
  A SoftBank Ventures Asia spokesperson told TechCrunch the fund decided to invest in SODA again because the company’s growth has increased rapidly since its previous funding. SODA’s post-money valuation is now about 24 billion JPY, or about $218 million USD.
 Part of SODA’s Series C funding will also be used to expand into other Asian markets, starting with Indonesia and the Philippines next year because both countries have growing e-commerce markets and a large percentage of Generation Zs, an ideal combination for SNKRDUNK.
   The company’s previous funding, its $22 million Series B, was announced in January. At the time, Uchiyama told TechCrunch demand for sneakers remained high despite the pandemic’s economic impact and increased adoption of online shopping also helped drive sales.
 SODA claims it hit record sales in May 2021, growing 900% year-over-year. Despite COVID-19, many sneaker C2C marketplaces, like  StockX, have also seen their sales increase.
 SNKRDUNK will work closely with KREAM to share knowledge about sneaker authentication, inventory management, logistics and other operations-related areas, with the goal of increasing their share of the Asian sneaker resell market.
 In addition to KREAM and SODA, SoftBank Ventures Asia is also an investor in China-based sneaker trading platform Nice.

note: 根据 Tofler 提供的监管文件，在线旅游应用程序 Ixigo 在其拟议的首次公开募股之前，已从新加坡主权财富基金领导的投资者那里筹集了 5300 万美元（39.5 亿卢比）。 “该公司正在为定于本日历年第四季度进行的首次公开募股寻求 750-8 亿美元的估值，”一位知情人士表示。 “它计划通过首次公开募股筹集 1,200-1,500 千万卢比，并将在下个月提交其红鲱鱼招股说明书草案。”文件显示，最新一轮的新股发行和一些现有投资者的二次出售也有其他投资者的参与，例如 Infoedge Venture Funds、White Oak、Bay Capital、Orios VenturePartners、Trifecta Capital 和 Malabar Investments。另一位了解情况的人士表示：“GIC 正在通过其新的公开市场基金进行投资，该基金旨在投资印度股票并支持 IPO 公司。” ET 早些时候曾报道 GIC 计划启动一个 30 亿美元的专注于印度的多头股票基金。 Ixigo 是 IPO 潮流的最新参与者，Paytm、CarTrade、Nykaa、PolicyBazaar 和 MobiKwik 等公司排队进入公开市场。外卖公司 Zomato 在上市期间的估值达到 65,000 千万卢比，这为更多科技初创公司在印度上市奠定了基础。根据文件显示，Ixigo 已通过出售新股筹集了 3600 万美元，而其余资金则用于让其早期支持者部分或全部退出。 Ixigo 得到了红杉资本、Elevation Capital、复星和 Micromax Informatics 等投资者的支持。 Ixigo 由 Aloke Bajpai 和 Rajnish Kumar 于 2007 年推出，拥有超过 2.5 亿的用户群。今年早些时候，该公司以未公开的价格收购了位于班加罗尔的在线火车发现和预订平台 Confirmtkt。在社交媒体上，Bajpai 在今年早些时候发布了关于他的基于人工智能的公司的表现的推文。他表示，这家总部位于 Gurugram 的公司的净收入为 15.3 亿卢比（同比增长 35%），营业亏损为 54 千万卢比，他说这是“87% 的盈利能力提升”。行业高管表示，随着大流行后新芽的出现，预计旅游和休闲行业将反弹并表现好于疫情爆发前的水平。 EaseMyTrip 是一家自助旅游搜索引擎提供商和 Ixigo 的竞争对手，于今年 3 月推出了 51 亿卢比的 IPO，在 NSE 以 212.25 卢比的价格上市，比其 187 卢比的发行价溢价 13.5%。周二，其股票收盘415 卢比。
note_en: Online travel app  Ixigo has raised $53 million (Rs 395 crore) from investors led by  Singapore sovereign wealth fund  , ahead of its proposed initial public offering, according to regulatory filings sourced from  Tofler.  “The company is looking at a valuation of $750-800 million for the IPO that is scheduled for the fourth quarter of the current calendar year,” said a person with knowledge of the development. “It plans to raise Rs 1,200-1,500 crore via its IPO for which it will file its draft red herring prospectus next month.”  The latest round —a mix of fresh issue of shares and secondary sale by some existing investors —also saw participation from other investors such as Infoedge Venture Funds,  White Oak, Bay Capital, Orios VenturePartners,  Trifecta Capital and Malabar Investments, the filings show.   “GIC is investing from its new public markets fund that it has created to invest in Indian equities and to back IPO-bound companies,” said another person with knowledge of the development.  ET had earlier  reported GIC’s plans to start a $3 billion India-focused long-only equities fund.  Ixigo is the latest entrant to the IPO bandwagon, with companies such as  Paytm,  CarTrade,  Nykaa,  PolicyBazaar and  MobiKwik lining up to tap the public market. Food delivery company  Zomato’s bumper listing commanding a valuation of Rs 65,000 crore during listing has set the ball rolling for more tech startups to list in India.  According to the filings, Ixigo has raised $36 million selling new shares, while the remaining has gone towards giving its early backers part or full exit. Ixigo is backed by investors such as Sequoia Capital, Elevation Capital, Fosun and Micromax Informatics.  Launched in 2007 by Aloke Bajpai and Rajnish Kumar, Ixigo has a user base of more than 250 million. Earlier this year, the company acquired Bengaluru-based online train discovery and booking platform Confirmtkt for an undisclosed amount.  Taking to social media, Bajpai had tweeted about his AI-based company’s performance earlier this year. He said the Gurugram-based company had earned net revenue of Rs 153 crore (35% Y-o-Y growth) and an operating loss of Rs5.4 crore, which he said was an “87% profitability improvement”.  With the emergence of green shoots post pandemic, the travel and leisure industry is expected to bounce back and perform better than pre-Covid levels, according to industry executives.  EaseMyTrip, a bootstrapped travel search engine provider and Ixigo’s competitor, had  launched its Rs 510 crore IPO in March this year, listing at Rs 212.25 on the NSE, a 13.5% premium to its issue price of Rs 187. On Tuesday, its shares closed at Rs 415.
title: 申请：印度在线旅游应用 Ixigo 拥有超过 2.5 亿用户，在新加坡 GIC 的牵头下筹集了 5300 万美元；资料来源：Ixigo 的目标是第四季度 IPO 的估值超过 7.5 亿美元
title_en: Filing: Ixigo, an Indian online travel app with 250M+ users, raises $53M led by Singapore's GIC; source: Ixigo is targeting a $750M+ valuation for its IPO in Q4

该轮融资由 Greenfield Partners 领投，Red Dot Capital Partners、StageOne Ventures、Eyal Ofer's – OG Tech、Javest Capital Partners、Maor Ventures 和 2B Angels 也参与其中。 C 轮融资是在公司 2500 万美元的 B 轮融资之后大约 10 个月到来的，据我们所知，Coralogix 的估值现在在 3 亿美元至 4 亿美元之间，这对初创公司来说是一个巨大的飞跃，因为它不断增长自去年这个时候以来增长了 250%，积累了大约 2,000 名付费客户，一些小团队每年只需支付 100 美元，而大型企业则每年支付 150 万美元。此前，Coralogix——在特拉维夫成立，总部也在旧金山——也筹集了 1000 万美元。 Coralogix 最初是作为一个平台，旨在为研发和工程团队提供质量保证支持。这里的重点是平台工程师的日志分析和指标，这仍然是其今天业务的重要组成部分。除此之外，近年来，Coralogix 的工具也被应用于云安全服务，通过提供一种观察数据的方法来观察通常可能指向违规或其他事件的任何不一致的数据，从而为公司的威胁情报做出贡献。 （为此目的，它与 Alien Vault 和其他人集成。）现在刚刚开始并将进一步开发的第三个领域——实际上是这项投资的用途之一——将开发如何将 Coralogix 用于商业智能.这是一个特别有趣的领域，因为它影响了 Coralogix 的构建方式，在数据被索引之前提供数据分析。 “这是关于大量但低价值的数据，”Coralogix 的首席执行官 Ariel Assaraf 在接受采访时说。 “客户不想存储数据 [或索引它]，但希望实时查看和可视化。我们开始看到一个用例，其中业务信息和我们的分析结合在一起用于情感分析和其他领域。”如今，有数十家强大的公司提供工具来涵盖日志分析和数据可观察性，突显了 DevOps 的总体增长和重要性。其中包括 DataDog、Sumo Logic、Splunk 等公司。
然而，Assaraf 认为，让他的公司与众不同的是它的方法：从本质上讲，它设计了一种在数据流被索引之前观察和分析的方法，使工程师可以更灵活地以不同方式查询数据，并且基本上收集更多洞察力，更快。他说，索引的另一个问题是它会影响延迟，这也会对组织的总体成本产生重大影响。对于 Coralogix 的许多竞争对手来说，扭转业务性质而不首先关注索引类似于完全重建业务，很难在他们的规模上做（尽管实际上这就是 Coralogix 所做的，当它作为一个小公司几年前，当时 Assaraf 担任首席执行官一职）。他认为可能更像是直接竞争对手的一家公司是 Confluent。 “我认为我们很快就会看到 Confluent 进入可观察性，因为它们具有流媒体功能，”他说，“但不是我们拥有的工具。”另一个即将出现的潜在竞争对手：Salesforce 及其进入该领域的潜力，突显了当今推动企业 IT 投资决策的流沙。 Assaraf 指出，Salesforce 已经拥有 Heroku、Slack 和 Tableau，这是开发人员用来跟踪和处理数据的三种主要工具，并且有强烈的传言称它试图收购 DataDog，“所以我们肯定会看到他们的发展方向。可以肯定的是，他们了解事情正在发生变化的方式。 Salesforce 刚开始时的所有预算都用于营销和销售。现在你卖给 IT。 Salesforce 了解向开发人员的转变，这就是他们要去的地方。”它为 Coralogix 等公司创造了一个非常有趣的前景和未来，投资者相信这家初创公司将继续塑造迄今为止的格局。 Greenfield Partners 的管理合伙人 Shay Grinfeld 表示：“数字化转型的巨大转变正在产生数据爆炸式增长，直到现在，这迫使企业在成本和覆盖范围之间做出决定。” “Coralogix 的实时流分析管道采用专有算法来打破这种权衡并显着节省成本。 Coralogix 建立了一个客户名册，其中包括世界上一些最大和最具创新性的公司。我们很高兴与 Ariel 和 Coralogix 团队合作，在他们重塑数据可观察性未来的旅程中。”
note_en:
The round was led by Greenfield Partners, with Red Dot Capital Partners, StageOne Ventures, Eyal Ofer’s – O.G. Tech, Janvest Capital Partners, Maor ventures, and 2B Angels also participating.
 This Series C is coming about 10 months after the company’s  Series B of $25 million, and from what we understand Coralogix’s valuation is now in the range of $300 million – $400 million, a big jump for the startup, coming on the back of it growing 250% since this time last year, racking up some 2,000 paying customers, some small teams paying as little as $100/year through to large enterprises paying $1.5 million/year.
  Previously, Coralogix — founded in Tel Aviv and with a HQ also in San Francisoc — had also raised a  round of $10 million.
 Coralogix got its start initially as a platform aimed at quality assurance support for R&D and engineering teams. The focus here is on log analytics and metrics for platform engineers, and this still forms a big part of its business today. Added to that, in recent years, Coralogix’s tools are also being applied to cloud security services, contributing to a company’s threat intelligence by providing a way to observe data for any inconsistencies that typically might point to a breach or another incident. (It integrates with Alien Vault and others for this purpose.)
 The third area that is just picking up now and will be developed further — one of the uses of this investment, in fact — will be to develop how Coralogix is used for business intelligence. This is a particularly interesting area because it plays into how Coralogix is built, to provide analytics on data before it is indexed.
 “It’s about high volume, but low value data,” Ariel Assaraf, Coralogix’s CEO, said in an interview. “Customers don’t want to store the data [or index it] but want to view it live and visualize it. We are starting to see a use case where business information and our analytics come together for sentiment analysis and other areas.”
 There are dozens of strong companies providing tools these days to cover log analytics and data observability, underscoring the general growth and importance of DevOps these days. They include companies like DataDog, Sumo Logic, Splunk and more.
 However, Assaraf believes that what sets his company apart from them is its approach: essentially it has devised a way of observing and analyzing data streams before they get indexed, giving engineers a more flexibility to query the data in different ways, and essentially glean more insights, faster. The other issue with indexing, he said, is that it impacts latency, which also has a big impact on overall costs for an organization.
 For many of Coralogix’s competitors, turning around the nature of the business to focus not first on indexing would be akin to completely rebuilding the business, hard to do at their scale (although in fact this is what Coralogix did, when it pivoted as a small company several years ago, which is when Assaraf took on the role of CEO). One company he believes might be more of a direct rival is Confluent.
 “I think we will see Confluent getting into observability very soon because they have the streaming capabilities,” he said, “but not the tools we have.” Another potential competitor looming on the horizon: Salesforce, and its potential move into that area, underscores the shifting sands of what is powering enterprise IT investment decisions today.
 Salesforce already has Heroku, Slack and Tableau, three major tools developers use for tracking and working with data, Assaraf pointed out, and there were strong rumors of it trying to buy DataDog, “so we definitely see where they are going. For sure, they understand the way things are changing. All the budgets when Salesforce first started were in marketing and sales. Now you sell to IT. Salesforce understands that shift to developers, and so that is where they are going.”
 It makes for a very interesting landscape and future for companies like Coralogix, one that investors believe the startup will continue to shape as it has up to now.
 “The   dramatic shift in digital transformation is generating an explosion of data, which until now has forced enterprises to decide between cost and coverage,” said Shay Grinfeld, managing partner at Greenfield Partners. “Coralogix’s real-time streaming analytics   pipeline employs proprietary algorithms to break this tradeoff and generate significant cost savings. Coralogix has built a customer roster that comprises some of the largest and most innovative companies in the world. We’re thrilled to partner with Ariel and the Coralogix team on their journey to reinvent the future of data observability.”

5 月 6 日，MONA SHTAYA 坐在她位于巴勒斯坦拉马拉的公寓里，懒洋洋地浏览推特时，她第一次注意到了一些不对劲。抗议浪潮正在东耶路撒冷蔓延，因为以色列定居者威胁要将 8 个巴勒斯坦家庭从他们位于 Sheikh Jarrah 的小社区的家园中驱逐出去。许多人在 Instagram 和 Twitter 上发布了他们的激进主义视频，但 Shtaya 开始看到巴勒斯坦人抱怨他们的账户被封锁，帖子被删除。一些人抱怨他们的 Instagram 故事档案正在消失，而另一些人则注意到他们故事的观看次数急剧下降。 Shtaya 与阿拉伯社交媒体发展中心（一个数字权利组织，通常被称为 7amleh）合作，对巴勒斯坦人的声音被数字平台压制并不陌生。以色列和统治加沙的激进组织哈马斯自 2007 年以来已经打了四场战争并多次交火； Shtaya 说，现场暴力的在线记录经常被删除，因为它们违反了有时不为人知的平台准则。她说，政府删除内容的要求也发挥了作用，她引用了 7amleh 的一份报告称，Facebook 接受了以色列网络部门提出的删除巴勒斯坦内容的请求的 81%，而 Twitter 根据以色列部提供的信息暂停了数十名巴勒斯坦用户。战略事务。但是，围绕 Sheikh Jarrah 抗议活动提出的审查要求数量超过了她之前遇到的任何情况——而 7amleh 恰好建立了正确的渠道，将这些投诉举报给社交媒体公司。 Shtaya 向下滚动到 Mohammed El-Kurd 的推文，他是一位年轻的巴勒斯坦活动家，他写道 Instagram 威胁要封锁他的帐户，并回答说：“如果你愿意，我们 7amleh 可以支持这一点。”她向其他抱怨社交媒体审查的巴勒斯坦人发送了类似的回复。几天后，7amleh 报告说它能够恢复一些帖子。从“阿拉伯之春”到“黑人的命也是命”运动，社交媒体平台已成为活动家聚集全球关注其事业的公共空间。对于长期以来一直被排除在主流对话之外的巴勒斯坦人来说，Instagram、Facebook、Twitter 和现在的 TikTok 提供了恢复他们的叙述、传播对他们处境的认识、拍摄与以色列安全部队的对抗以及记录暴力镇压的方法。抗议。然而，正如 Shtaya 指出的那样，平台本身并不总是中立的。尽管 Instagram 在 5 月 6 日晚发布了一条过错，将故事、精彩片段和档案的删除归咎于世界各地发生的技术问题，但 Shtaya 指出，这些故障并不能解释她当天标记的所有案例。更重要的是，Shtaya 表示，在 Instagram 于 5 月 7 日表示已解决问题后，巴勒斯坦人继续面临发帖困难。 ，“ 她说。 “所以他们的宣布对巴勒斯坦人来说毫无意义。”
Facebook 承认巴勒斯坦人遇到的问题超出了影响世界各地 Instagram 故事的技术错误，并引用了一个错误，该错误暂时限制了在阿克萨清真寺主题标签页面上查看内容。 “虽然这两个问题都已解决，但它们本来就不应该发生。 Facebook 的一位发言人表示，对于那些认为自己无法引起人们对重要事件的关注，或者认为这是故意压抑自己声音的人，我们感到非常抱歉。从 5 月 6 日到本月底，随着耶路撒冷的冲突演变成以色列和哈马斯之间的战争，最终以临时停火告终，7amleh 表示，它总共记录了 668 起侵犯数字权利的事件，并在社交媒体上标记了每起事件平台，仅 Instagram 就有 250 多起投诉。然而，平台的反应各不相同。 Shtaya 说 Twitter 是响应最快的，7amleh 提到的违规行为中有 96% 在短时间内得到解决。 Twitter 发言人表示，在冲突开始时，其自动反垃圾邮件系统“对一些比我们的政策预期更严重的账户采取了执法行动。”但经过迅速审查后，Twitter 在适当的情况下“主动恢复了对受影响帐户的访问权限”。与此同时，Shtaya 表示，在 6 月中旬之前，只有 20% 的涉及 Instagram 及其母公司 Facebook 的案件得到解决。 Facebook对这些案件没有得到及时审查的说法提出异议。 “我们从 7amleh 收到的所有报告都在几天内得到了优先排序、审查和解决。如果内容一开始不应该被删除，我们会立即恢复它。然而，他们提交的一些案例中包含的内容确实违反了我们的社区标准，或者在我们的平台上完全活跃而没有受到任何限制，”Facebook 发言人说。 Facebook 还表示，它有一个专门的团队，其中包括阿拉伯语和希伯来语人士，“专注于确保我们正在删除有害内容，同时尽快解决任何执法错误。”然而，对许多人来说，这还不够快。随着大量帖子被删除，精通技术的阿拉伯语用户想出了各种方法来规避算法审查的威胁：他们用无点阿拉伯语、阿拉伯语和拉丁语混合文字书写，或者玩弄字母顺序来绕过内容审核系统。一群朋友甚至推出了一个网站，将阿拉伯文字编码成图画，以阻止社交媒体平台删除帖子。但这些措施只是短期解决方案，并不能解决 Shtaya 对社交媒体公司以牺牲巴勒斯坦人为代价删除内容的压倒性担忧。她将 7amleh 记录的数百个 Instagram 和 Facebook 帖子删除与以色列极端主义团体在 WhatsApp 上传播的错误信息和对暴力的呼吁进行了对比。 Shtaya 说，如果 Facebook 的社区准则同样适用，“针对巴勒斯坦人和阿拉伯人的煽动和仇恨言论将被自动删除。” （Facebook 指出它无法访问个人 WhatsApp 聊天，并补充说“当向我们报告信息时，我们会采取行动禁止我们认为可能会造成迫在眉睫的伤害的帐户。”）
“社交平台基本上都是海外的缺席房东。他们在世界上发布他们的产品，然后他们就看看会发生什么。” FACEBOOK 和 TWITTER 曾因其在阿拉伯之春期间帮助活动家组织起来的作用而受到称赞。然而，今天，社交平台正在与多个方面的审查指控作斗争。 Facebook 的情况尤其如此，它在 Instagram、WhatsApp 及其其他应用程序系列中每月拥有 34.5 亿活跃用户。 Black Lives Matter 活动人士此前曾抱怨该平台阻止了其围绕种族主义的对话。去年年底，国际特赦组织称 Facebook 是审查越南政府批评者的同谋。哈佛法学院讲师、专门研究在线平台全球监管的博士生伊芙琳·杜埃克 (Evelyn Douek) 表示：“长期以来，平台基本上都是海外的房东。” “他们有点像在世界上发布他们的产品，然后他们只是看看会发生什么。”但在过去十年中，Facebook 和 Twitter 面临着检查垃圾邮件、错误信息和恐怖主义传播的巨大压力。 Douek 表示，缅甸军方特别使用 Facebook 来推动对罗兴亚穆斯林的种族灭绝，这是一个关键的转折点。 “几乎没有国会听证会提到 [Facebook 在缅甸的角色] 没有出现，”杜埃克说。 “这种对海外平台运营的真正关注。”然而，将 Facebook 等平台的观念从言论自由的典型代表转变为压制言论的建筑师，需要的不仅仅是监管重点。要理解这一点，值得研究内容审核的“黑匣子”世界。 Facebook 中东和北非政策团队前负责人 Ashraf Zeitoon 表示，Facebook 依靠算法和人工审核员的组合来浏览平台上的大量内容。
但是这些方法并不总是正确的。计算机算法可能是区分不可接受的文字和图像的不完美工具，尤其是在处理西方文化之外的区域背景时。人类版主也存在同样的问题，他们只有几秒钟的时间来决定帖子是否符合平台准则。 Facebook 首席执行官马克·扎克伯格此前估计，该平台的版主犯的错误超过十分之一，用户几乎没有办法对此类决定提出上诉。一个特别引人注目的例子发生在 5 月 7 日，以色列警察冲进阿克萨清真寺，投掷眩晕手榴弹，迫使巴勒斯坦人四散开来。希望记录暴力的巴勒斯坦人用阿拉伯语转录的阿克萨语 (الأقصى) 标记了他们的 Instagram 故事，却发现点击标签的用户无法看到他们的帖子。在 BuzzFeed 报告称，错误发生是因为一名人类主持人将清真寺误认为是美国境内被指定为恐怖组织的阿克萨烈士旅，这引发了愤怒。 （Facebook 的一位发言人表示，该限制在公司意识到错误后的两小时内解除。）电子前沿基金会国际言论自由主任吉莉安·约克表示，版主准备跳枪是危险但可以理解的冲动。 “这些公司害怕违反美国法律，”约克解释说。 “我理解为什么公司最终会犯这些错误，尽管这绝对是令人震惊的。” Facebook 内容审核系统的最后一个齿轮是其区域政策团队，Zeitoon 将其称为所在领域的“特警团队”。他们的许多职责包括确定平台上允许的内容的准则，并且当出现问题或争议时，他们通常在检查帖子时拥有最后决定权。尽管 Facebook 的区域存在使其能够制定更了解区域背景的政策，但 Zeitoon 表示，其内容审核过程也容易受到不同的偏见来源的影响，因为其实地操作人员可能会受到政治压力和商业压力的影响。激励措施。 Facebook 表示，其政策团队具有本地“理解力”，但他们的最终目标是“为 Facebook 的利益服务”。随着平台越来越多地陷入地方政治和地缘政治问题，Zeitoon 表示 Facebook 需要对其内容删除政策更加开放，以确保错误不会重演。 “Facebook 不会披露他们收到的内容删除或内容删除的 [政府数量] 请求，因此您在科技公司透明度报告中看到的任何内容都只是冰山一角，”他说。哈佛大学的 Douek 指出了以色列和巴勒斯坦的历史权力动态，以及 Facebook 与以色列政府和巴勒斯坦官员之间关系之间所谓的差异，这是两者之间内容审核继续不平衡的原因。
“以色列采取了这些非正式的报告措施，并对平台施加了很大的压力，要求其删除大量内容，”杜埃克观察到。 “并且平台在这样做时对以色列政府相当友好，这显然为正在发生的事情创造了一种非常不同的背景条件。” Facebook 对这种描述提出异议。 “与世界其他国家相比，我们在以色列限制的内容要少得多。我们不会也不会从 Facebook 或 Instagram 删除不违反我们社区标准的内容——任何关于我们在以色列政府压力下这样做的建议都是完全不准确的，”发言人说。该公司还反驳了更广泛的说法，即它不是中立的，并表示它与巴勒斯坦组织有着“长期”的关系，包括参与巴勒斯坦数字激进主义论坛。数字权利组织 Access Now 的中东和北非政策经理 Marwa Fatafta 指出透明度是社交媒体平台向前发展的关键。 Fatafta 说：“我们知道您的系统运行异常，错误当然会发生。” “让我们谈谈结构性问题，让我们谈谈透明度。” Fatafta 补充说，谈到改革，一切都始于做出这些改变的意愿。 “[对于]这些公司，他们的作案手法是利润高于人，”她说。 Fatafta 表示，Facebook 应该与组织合作，以确保在设计代码和政策时考虑到地区的细微差别和背景。她还建议平台运营商聘请一组“专门的人类版主”，他们可以扩大 Facebook 内部上诉程序的访问范围，并对某条内容是否真的违反社区标准做出最终决定。 Fatafta 说：“如果你认真对待人权和透明度，就不乏解决方案。”全球压力一直建立在社交媒体公司的基础上，以消除其固有的偏见。
在谢赫贾拉 (Sheikh Jarrah) 动乱之后，Facebook 尤其面临公共关系危机。为了抗议审查报告，亲巴勒斯坦的活动人士发起了一场运动，将 Facebook 的评论在 Apple App Store 上的评分降至 2.3 颗星，在 Google Play 上将其降至 2.4 颗星。 NBC 新闻报道称，该问题在 Facebook 内被归类为“严重性”问题，因为员工对用户信任度急剧下降表示担忧。在公司内部，Facebook 员工也对泄露的文件感到困扰，这些文件详细说明了公司将“犹太复国主义”一词视为反犹太言论的政策，他们说这可能会阻碍对以色列国家的批评。据英国《金融时报》报道，大约 200 名员工签署了一封公开信，呼吁 Facebook 的领导人解决对该平台的内容审核系统压制亲巴勒斯坦声音的担忧。围绕社交媒体平台的喧嚣引起了足够的关注，进入了美国国会的办公室。密歇根州第 13 区的代表拉希达·特莱布 (Rashida Tlaib) 给 Facebook、Twitter 和 TikTok 的政策负责人写了一封信，询问了一系列旨在使他们的内容政策更加透明的问题。特莱布还指责 Facebook 在审核以色列和巴勒斯坦内容时表现出“令人不安的双重标准”。 “虽然分享巴勒斯坦观点的帖子和账户受​​到审查和限制，但以色列极端主义团体公开使用 Facebook 旗下的 WhatsApp 来协调对巴勒斯坦人的暴力暴徒袭击，”她写道，并引用了《纽约时报》的一篇报道。 “我无法理解 Facebook 如何为审查巴勒斯坦和平声音的行为辩护，同时为极端主义仇恨提供一个组织平台。”这些平台在一定程度上做出了回应。 Facebook 改变了 Instagram 显示其内容的方式，并发布了其首个企业人权政策，承诺该公司采取措施，例如挑战破坏端到端加密的企图，以及保护人权捍卫者。这场冲突还促使 Facebook 在该地区的参与发生了变化。在以色列和加沙之间的最新暴力浪潮中，Facebook 会见了以色列国防部长本尼·甘茨 (Benny Gantz) 和巴勒斯坦总理穆罕默德·什塔耶 (Mohammad Shtayyeh)，讨论平台监管问题。 Facebook 表示：“这些会议旨在确保各方了解公司已采取并将继续采取的措施，以确保平台安全。”出席与巴勒斯坦官员会面的 7amleh 的 Shtaya 将 Facebook 重新致力于人权视为一个积极的信号。也就是说，她正在等待他们真正兑现他们的承诺。 “我们希望看到他们的行动，”Shtaya 说。
这个故事在阿联酋时间 7 月 28 日下午 2 点 18 分更新，包括来自 Facebook 的额外回复，出现在第 11 段中。
note_en:
MONA SHTAYA WAS sitting in her apartment in Ramallah, Palestine on May 6, idly browsing through Twitter, when she first noticed something amiss. A wave of protests was spreading across East Jerusalem, as Israeli settlers threatened to displace eight Palestinian families from their homes in the tiny neighborhood of Sheikh Jarrah. Many posted footage of their activism on Instagram and Twitter, but Shtaya was beginning to see Palestinians complain of their accounts getting blocked and posts removed. Some complained that their Instagram story archives were disappearing, while others noticed a steep drop in the number of views on their stories.
 Shtaya, who works with the Arab Center for Social Media Advancement (a digital rights organization more commonly known as 7amleh), is no stranger to Palestinian voices being silenced by digital platforms. Israel and Hamas, the militant group that rules Gaza, have fought four wars and exchanged fire multiple times since 2007; Shtaya says online records of on-the-ground violence are often wiped because they violate sometimes-obscure platform guidelines.
  Government requests to take down content also play a role, she says, citing a 7amleh report that said Facebook accepted 81 percent of requests made by Israel’s Cyber Unit to remove Palestinian content, while Twitter suspended dozens of Palestinian users based of information from Israel’s Ministry of Strategic Affairs.
 But the number of censorship claims around the Sheikh Jarrah protests exceeded anything she’d encountered before—and it just so happened that 7amleh had set up the right channels to flag those complaints to social media companies. Shtaya scrolled down to a tweet by Mohammed El-Kurd, a young Palestinian activist who wrote that Instagram was threatening to block his account, and replied, “we at 7amleh can support in that, if you want.” She sent similar replies to other Palestinians complaining of social media censorship. A few days later, 7amleh reported that it was able to restore some of the posts.
 From the Arab Spring to the  Black Lives Matter movement, social media platforms have become a public space for activists to rally global attention to their cause. For Palestinians—whose voices have long been left out of mainstream conversations—Instagram, Facebook, Twitter, and now TikTok have provided ways to reclaim their narratives, spreading awareness of their situations, filming confrontations with the Israeli security forces, and documenting violent clampdowns on protests.
 However, as Shtaya points out, the platforms themselves aren’t always neutral. Although Instagram posted a mea culpa late on May 6, blaming the removal of stories, highlights, and archives on technical problems occurring around the world, Shtaya points out that the glitches don’t explain all the cases she flagged that day. More importantly, Shtaya says that Palestinians continued to face difficulties posting after Instagram said that it had resolved the problems on May 7.
 “Sixty-eight percent of the cases that we have received on Instagram were after the platform announced that they solved their technical glitches,” she says. “So their announcement was somehow meaningless for Palestinians.”
 Facebook acknowledged that the issues experienced by Palestinians went beyond the technical bug affecting Instagram stories around the world, citing an error that briefly restricted content from being viewed on the Al Aqsa Mosque hashtag page. “While both issues have been fixed, they never should have happened in the first place. We’re so sorry to everyone who felt they couldn’t bring attention to important events, or who felt this was a deliberate suppression of their voice,” says a Facebook spokesperson.
 From May 6 through the end of the month, as the conflict in Jerusalem descended into a war between Israel and Hamas that ended in a temporary ceasefire, 7amleh says it logged a total of 668 digital rights violations, each of which it flagged to social media platforms, with more than 250 complaints relating to Instagram alone.
  Reactions from the platforms varied, however. Shtaya says Twitter was the most responsive, with 96 percent of the alleged violations 7amleh referred to it resolved within a short period of time. A Twitter spokesperson says that, at the onset of the conflict, its automated anti-spam systems “took enforcement action on a number of accounts that was more severe than intended under our policies.” But after swift reviews, Twitter “proactively reinstated access to the affected accounts” where appropriate.
 Meanwhile, Shtaya says only 20 percent of the cases involving Instagram and its parent company Facebook had been resolved by mid-June. Facebook disputes the suggestion that these cases were not promptly reviewed. “All the reports we received from 7amleh were prioritized, reviewed, and resolved within a matter of days. In instances where the content shouldn’t have been removed in the first place, we restored it straight away. However some of the cases they submitted included content that indeed violates our community standards or was fully active on our platforms with no restrictions applied,” a Facebook spokesperson says.
 Facebook also says it has a dedicated team, which includes Arabic and Hebrew speakers, “focused on making sure we’re removing harmful content, while addressing any enforcement errors as quickly as possible.”
 Yet, for many, that was not quick enough. As numerous posts were removed, tech-savvy Arabic-speaking users came up with ways to dodge the threat of censorship-by-algorithm: they wrote in dotless Arabic, mixed Arabic and Latin script, or played around with the order of letters to bypass content-moderation systems. One group of friends even launched a website that encoded Arabic text into drawings to stop social media platforms from removing posts.
 But such measures are only short-term solutions, and don’t resolve Shtaya’s overwhelming concern about social media companies removing content at the expense of Palestinians. She contrasts the hundreds of Instagram and Facebook post takedowns documented by 7amleh with the misinformation and calls to violence spread by Israeli extremist groups on WhatsApp. If Facebook’s community guidelines were equally applied, Shtaya says, “the incitement and hate speech against Palestinians and Arabs would be taken down automatically.” (Facebook points out that it does not have access to personal WhatsApp chats, adding that “when information is reported to us, we take action to ban accounts we believe may be involved in causing imminent harm.”)
 “Social platforms have basically been absentee landlords overseas. They unleash their products on the world and then they just see what happens.”
 FACEBOOK AND TWITTER were once hailed for their role in helping activists organize during the Arab Spring. Today, however, social platforms are battling censorship accusations on multiple fronts. That’s especially the case with Facebook, which has 3.45 billion monthly active users across Instagram, WhatsApp, and the rest of its app family. Black Lives Matter activists have previously complained that the platform has blocked its conversations around racism. And late last year, Amnesty International called Facebook complicit in censoring critics of the Vietnamese government.
 “Platforms, for a long time, have basically been absentee landlords overseas,” says Evelyn Douek, a lecturer at Harvard Law School, and doctoral student specializing in studying the global regulation of online platforms. “They sort of unleash their products on the world and then they just see what happens.”
 But over the course of the past decade, Facebook and Twitter have come under immense pressure to check the spread of spam, misinformation, and terrorism. The Myanmar military’s use of Facebook, in particular, to fuel a genocide against Rohingya Muslims, was a key turning point, according to Douek.
 “There’s barely a congressional hearing that goes by where [Facebook’s role in Myanmar] doesn’t come up,” says Douek. “That sort of really supercharged focus on platform operations overseas.”
 However, it took more than regulatory focus to transform perceptions of platforms like Facebook from poster children of free speech to architects of its suppression. To understand that, it’s worth examining the “black box” world of content moderation.
 Facebook relies on a mix of algorithms and human moderators to sweep through the vast amount of content on the platform, according to Ashraf Zeitoon, the former head of Facebook’s policy team in the Middle East and North Africa.
 But these methods don’t always get it right. Computer algorithms can be imperfect tools for distinguishing between unacceptable words and images, especially when it comes to dealing with regional contexts outside Western culture. The same problem exists with human moderators, who have mere seconds to decide whether or not a post meets the platform guidelines. Facebook chief executive Mark Zuckerberg has previously estimated that the platform’s moderators get more than one in ten cases wrong, and users have little recourse to appeal such decisions.
 A particularly striking example occurred as Israeli police stormed through the Al Aqsa Mosque on May 7, throwing stun grenades and forcing Palestinians to scatter. Palestinians looking to document the violence tagged their Instagram stories with the Arabic transcription of Al Aqsa (الأقصى), only to find that users that clicked on the hashtag weren’t able to see their posts. Outrage grew after BuzzFeed reported that the error occurred because a human moderator mistook the mosque for the Al Aqsa Martyrs’ Brigade, designated as a terrorist organization within the US. (A Facebook spokesperson says the restriction was lifted within two hours of the company becoming aware of the error.)
 Jillian York, director of international freedom of expression at the Electronic Frontier Foundation, says the moderator’s readiness to jump the gun is a dangerous but understandable impulse. “These companies are terrified of violating US law,” York explains. “I understand why companies end up making those kinds of mistakes, even though it’s absolutely egregious.”
 The final gears in Facebook’s content moderation system are its regional policy teams, which Zeitoon calls the “SWAT teams” of their field. Their many responsibilities include determining the guidelines for what’s allowed on the platform, and they usually have the last word in checking posts when a problem or controversy emerges.
 Although Facebook’s regional presence allows it to devise policies that are more informed of regional context, Zeitoon says its content-moderation process is also vulnerable to a different source of bias, as its on-the-ground operatives might be influenced by political pressure and commercial incentives. Facebook says its policy teams have local “understanding,” but that their ultimate objective is “to serve the interests of Facebook.”
 As platforms get increasingly mired in both local politics and geopolitical issues, Zeitoon says Facebook needs to be more open about its content removal policies to ensure mistakes don’t get repeated. “Facebook doesn’t disclose the [number of government] requests they receive for content takedown or content removal, so whatever you see in tech company transparency reports is the tip of the iceberg,” he says.
 Harvard’s Douek points toward the historical power dynamics in Israel and Palestine, and alleged differences between Facebook’s relationships with the Israeli government and Palestinian officials, as reasons why content moderation continues to be uneven between the two.
 “Israel has these informal reporting measures and places a lot of pressure on the platform to take down a lot of content,” Douek observes. “And platforms have been reasonably friendly to the Israeli government in doing that, which obviously creates a very different sort of background condition to what’s going on.”
 Facebook disputes that characterization. “We restrict much less content in Israel than in other countries around the world. We do not and would not remove content from Facebook or Instagram that doesn’t violate our Community Standards—and any suggestion that we do so under pressure from the Israeli government is completely inaccurate,” the spokesperson says.
 The company also pushed back against broader claims that it isn’t neutral, and says that it has a “longstanding” relationship with Palestinian organizations, including participation in the Palestine Digital Activism Forum.
 Marwa Fatafta, the policy manager for the Middle East and North Africa at digital rights organization Access Now, points to transparency as key for social media platforms to move forward.
 “We get it that your systems are running amok, and errors are of course bound to happen,” says Fatafta. “Let’s talk about structural issues, and let’s talk about transparency.” When it comes to reform, it all begins with the will to make those changes, Fatafta adds. “[With] these companies, their modus operandi is profits over people,” she says.
 Fatafta says that Facebook should be working with organizations to ensure it takes regional nuances and context into account when it comes to designing code and policies. She also suggests that platform operators hire a group of “specialized human moderators” who can broaden access to an appeals process within Facebook, and make a final call on whether a piece of content actually violated community standards. “If you’re serious about human rights and transparency, there’s no shortage of solutions,” says Fatafta.
  GLOBAL PRESSURE HAS been building on social media companies to route out their inherent biases.
 Facebook, in particular, faced a public relations crisis after the unrest over Sheikh Jarrah. To protest the censorship reports, pro-Palestinian activists launched a campaign that tanked Facebook’s reviews to 2.3 stars on Apple’s App Store and 2.4 stars on Google Play. NBC News reported that the issue was categorized as a “severity one” issue within Facebook, as employees expressed concern over a steep drop in user trust.
 Within the company, Facebook employees were also troubled by leaked documents detailing the company’s policy of treating the term “Zionist” as anti-Semitic speech, which they said could impede criticism of the Israeli state. Around 200 employees have signed an open letter calling for Facebook’s leaders to address concerns that pro-Palestine voices are being suppressed by the platform’s content moderation systems, according to the  Financial Times.
 The uproar around social media platforms attracted enough attention to enter the offices of US Congress. Rashida Tlaib, a representative for Michigan’s 13th district, penned a letter to the policy heads of Facebook, Twitter, and TikTok asking a series of questions designed to make their content policies more transparent.
 Tlaib also chastised Facebook for displaying a “disturbing double-standard” when it came to moderating Israeli and Palestinian content. “While posts and accounts sharing Palestinian perspectives are censored and restricted, extremist Israeli groups openly use Facebook-owned WhatsApp to coordinate violent mob attacks on Palestinians,” she wrote, citing a report from the New York Times. “I cannot understand how Facebook can justify censoring peaceful Palestinian voices while providing an organizing platform for extremist hate.”
 The platforms are responding, to a degree. Facebook has made changes to the way Instagram displays its content, and released its first-ever corporate human rights policy, committing the company to measures like challenging attempts to undermine end-to-end encryption, and protecting human rights defenders.
 The conflict has also spurred changes in Facebook’s engagement in the region. During the latest wave of violence between Israel and Gaza, Facebook met with both the Israeli defense minister Benny Gantz and Palestinian prime minister Mohammad Shtayyeh, to discuss platform regulation. “These meetings were an effort to ensure all parties were aware of the steps that the company has taken, and will continue to take, to keep the platform safe,” Facebook says.
 Shtaya of 7amleh, who was present at the meeting with Palestinian officials, takes Facebook’s renewed commitment to human rights as a positive sign. That said, she is waiting for them to actually follow through on their promises. “We want to see their actions,” says Shtaya .
 This story was updated at 2.18pm UAE time on July 28, to include an additional response from Facebook, which appears in paragraph 11.
title: 采访中东社交媒体倡导和数字权利组织 7amleh 的 Mona Shtaya，了解社交网络如何在 5 月审查巴勒斯坦人
title_en: Interview with Mona Shtaya of 7amleh, a social media advocacy and digital rights group in the Middle East, on how social networks censored Palestinians in May

这轮融资中有许多知名人士，这既表明大型风投现在对 SMB 市场的重视程度如何，也符合参与其中的个人的战略利益。纪源资本领投这轮融资，过去的支持者贝恩资本风险投资公司、基线风险投资公司、基岩投资公司、牛仔风险投资公司和科斯拉风险投资公司也参与其中。与此同时，个人包括 Focus Brands 总裁 Kat Cole、Jocelyn Mangan（PapaJohns 和 Chownow 的董事会成员以及 Snag 的前首席运营官）、薪资和福利公司的前首席财务官 Gusto Mike Dinsdale、Guild Education 创始人 Rachel Carlson、明星运动员 Jrue 和 Lauren Holiday好吧好吧好吧演员和着名的普通人和未来的政治候选人马修麦康纳。资金是在 Homebase 强劲增长之后获得的（不要与同名的英国/爱尔兰家居装修连锁店，也不要与 YC 支持的越南 proptech 初创公司混淆）。该公司现在在其平台上拥有大约 100,000 家小型企业，共有 100 万名员工，这些企业使用 Homebase 管理与按小时支付的工人相关的各种活动，包括（最近）工资单，以及轮班安排，时钟和时间表、招聘和入职、沟通和人力资源合规性。 Homebase 创始人兼首席执行官约翰·沃尔德曼 (John Waldmann) 表示，这笔资金将用于继续吸引更多客户，并扩大向他们提供的服务列表，其中可能包括更多面向一线和服务人员的功能，以及作为小型企业的功能，他们可能还有一些可能仍然每小时工作的“办公桌”工人。沃尔德曼说，共同点不是这些工作的确切性质，而是所有这些工作，部分原因是每小时方面，到目前为止，技术在很大程度上未能提供服务。 “从一开始，我们的使命就是帮助当地企业及其团队，”他说。他说他的部分灵感来自他认识的人：一个儿时的朋友，他拥有一家独立的、不断扩大的连锁餐厅，正在经历管理团队的挑战，在纸上完成他的大部分工作；和他在酒店工作的姐姐，这与他餐厅朋友的挑战并没有什么不同。她不得不打电话来看看她什么时候工作，在笔记本上写下她的工作时间，以确保她得到准确的报酬。 “有很多科技公司专注于让坐在电脑或办公桌前的人们更轻松地工作，但正在为其他人构建工具，”沃尔德曼说。 “在工作世界中，体验与技术不同。”
Homebase 目前专注于北美市场——仅在美国就有大约 500 万家小企业，因此那里有很多机会。他们中的许多人在 Covid-19 生活的最后 18 个月中经历了巨大的压力，导致一些人完全关闭，也让他们将注意力集中在如何更有效地、更有组织地管理和开展工作上，以确保您知道您的员工在哪里，并且您的员工始终知道应该做什么。有趣的是，随着时间的推移，Homebase 为其平台添加了哪些类型的服务：在某种程度上，这表明时薪工人如何成为劳动力中更加复杂和突出的方面，并具有自己独特的需求。例如，现在已在 27 个州使用的 Payroll 还附带预付款，这为 Homebase 提供了其他类型的金融服务。 “小型企业是美国经济的命脉，超过 60% 的美国人受雇于我们 3000 万家小型企业之一。在大流行后的世界中，技术对包括中小型企业在内的各种规模的企业而言从未如此重要，”GGV Capital 的 aartner 管理人员兼 Homebase 董事会新成员杰夫理查兹在一份声明中说。 “多年来，Homebase 的团队不知疲倦地工作，以一种有助于提高盈利能力、更好的招聘和增长的方式将技术带给中小企业。我们很高兴看到 Homebase 在美国的小企业复苏中发挥如此重要的作用，也很高兴成为未来使命的一部分。”看到麦康纳参与这一轮很有趣，因为他最近转向政治，计划在 2022 年竞选德克萨斯州州长。我们，”他在一份声明中说。 “它们在赋予我们城市生计、身份和社区意识方面发挥着重要作用。这就是我投资 Homebase 的原因。 Homebase 将小型企业运营带入现代时代，并帮助全国各地的人们不仅继续更努力地工作，而且更聪明地工作。”
note_en:
The round has a number of big names in it that are as much a sign of how large VCs are valuing the SMB market right now, as it is of the strategic interest of the individuals who are also participating. GGV Capital is leading the round, with past backers Bain Capital Ventures, Baseline Ventures, Bedrock, Cowboy Ventures, and Khosla Ventures also participating. Individuals meanwhile include president of Focus Brands Kat Cole, Jocelyn Mangan (a board member at PapaJohns and Chownow and former COO of Snag), former CFO of payroll and benefits company Gusto Mike Dinsdale, Guild Education founder Rachel Carlson, star athletes Jrue and Lauren Holiday and alright alright alright actor and famous everyman and future political candidate Matthew McConaughey.
   The funding is coming on the heels of strong growth for Homebase (which is not to be confused with the UK/Irish  home improvement chain of the same name, nor the  YC-backed Vietnamese proptech startup).
 The company now has some 100,000 small businesses, with 1 million employees in total, on its platform, which use Homebase to manage all manner of activities related to workers that are paid hourly, including (most recently) payroll, as well as shift scheduling, timeclocks and timesheets, hiring and onboarding, communication, and HR compliance.
 John Waldmann, Homebase’s founder and CEO, said the funding will go towards both continuing to bring on more customers, as well as expand the list of services offered to them, which could include more features geared to front-line and service workers, as well as features for small businesses who might also have some “desk” workers who might still work hourly.
 The common thread, Waldmann said, is not the exact nature of those jobs, but the fact that all of them, partly because of that hourly aspect, have been largely underserved by tech up to now.
 “From the beginning, our mission was to help local businesses and their teams,” he said. Part of his inspiration he said came from people he knew: a childhood friend who owned an independent, expanding restaurant chain, and was going through the challenges of managing his teams there, carrying out most of his work on paper; and his sister who  worked in hospitality, which didn’t look all that different from his restaurant friend’s challenges. She had to call in to see when she was working, writing her hours in a notebook to make sure she got paid accurately.
 “There are a lot of tech companies focused on making work easier for folks that sit at computers or desks, but are building tools for these others,” Waldmann said. “In the world of work, the experience just looks different with technology.”
 Homebase currently is focused on the North American market — there are some 5 million small businesses in the U.S. alone, and so there is a lot of opportunity there. The  huge pressure that many them have experienced in the last 18 months of Covid-19 living, leading some to shut down altogether, has also focused the mind on how to manage and carry out work much more efficiently and in a more organized way to ensure you know where your staff is, and that your staff knows what it should be doing at all times.
 What will be interesting is to see what kinds of services Homebase adds to its platform over time: in a way it’s a sign of how the hourly wage workers are becoming a more sophisticated and salient aspect of the workforce, with their own unique demands. Payroll, which is now live in 27 states, also comes with pay advances, opening the door to other kinds of financial services for Homebase, for example.
 “Small businesses are the lifeblood of the American economy, with more than 60% of Americans employed by one of our 30 million small businesses. In a post-pandemic world, technology has never been more important to businesses of all sizes, including SMBs,” said Jeff Richards, managing aartner at GGV Capital and new Homebase board member, in a statement. “The team at Homebase has worked tirelessly for years to bring technology to SMBs in a way that helps drive increased profitability, better hiring and growth. We’re thrilled to see Homebase playing such an important role in America’s small business recovery and thrilled to be part of the mission going forward.”
 It’s interesting to see McConaughey involved in this round, given that he’s most recently made a turn towards politics, with plans to run for governor of Texas in 2022. “Hard working people who work in and run restaurants and local businesses are important to all of us,” he said in a statement. “They play an important role in giving our cities a sense of livelihood, identity, and community. This is why I’ve invested in Homebase. Homebase brings small business operations into the modern age and helps folks across the country not only continue to work harder, but work smarter.”
title: 消息人士称，Homebase 提供管理中小型企业团队和小时工的工具，筹集了 7000 万美元的 C 轮融资，由纪源资本领投，估值为 5 亿至 6 亿美元
title_en: Homebase, which offers tools to manage teams and hourly workers at SMBs, raises $70M Series C led by GGV Capital, sources say at a $500M-$600M valuation
